notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Piping Fundamentals and Design
notes.content:
PIPING FUNDAMENTALS

PURPOSE AND FUNCTION:
• Transports fluids (and sometimes fluid/solid mixtures) between equipment
• Design must account for temperature, pressure, flow rate, chemical/physical properties, contamination, and shock loads

DESIGN FACTORS:
• Temperature: affects material properties, expansion, thermal stress
• Pressure: determines wall thickness, material selection, support design
• Flow rate/velocity: affects erosion, corrosion, and pressure drop
• Shock loads: water hammer, steam hammer, thermal shock, seismic events require robust design
• Change of direction: requires proper support and restraint

MATERIAL SELECTION:
• Low-carbon steel: economical, prone to rust/corrosion
• Low-alloy steel: high-temperature applications, improved wear/fatigue/corrosion resistance
• Stainless steel: corrosion resistant, minimizes corrosion product buildup
• Copper alloys: excellent corrosion resistance, good conductivity
• Nickel alloys: high temperature/pressure applications, high cost
• Plastic: corrosion resistant, lightweight, limited temperature range

PIPE SUPPORT SYSTEMS:
• Rigid restraints: fixed, prevent movement
• Spring hangers: support vertical gravity loading, allow thermal movement
• Snubbers: limit seismic motion while allowing thermal growth

THERMAL EFFECTS:
• Thermal loops/bends: accommodate thermal expansion
• Expansion joints: allow controlled linear expansion
• Insulation: controls heat loss/gain and protects personnel

notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Valve Functions and Basic Construction
notes.content:
FIVE PRINCIPAL VALVE FUNCTIONS:

1. Starting/Stopping Flow
   - Isolation: completely stop or start fluid flow
   - Examples: gate valves, ball valves, plug valves

2. Throttling Flow
   - Control flow rate without stopping completely
   - Requires a throttling-capable design
   - Examples: globe valves, needle valves

3. Controlling Direction of Flow
   - Routes fluid to different paths
   - Examples: directional control valves, check valves

4. Regulating Pressure
   - Maintains desired system pressure
   - Examples: pressure regulators, pressure-reducing valves

5. Relieving Pressure
   - Protection against overpressure
   - Examples: relief valves, safety valves

BASIC VALVE CONSTRUCTION:
• Body: contains the internal flow path, seats, and internal parts
• Bonnet: upper section that seals the body
• Disk: moves to open/close or throttle flow
• Seat rings: provide seal surface for the disk
• Stem: connects disk to actuator/handwheel
• Backseat: allows packing adjustment without system depressurization
• Packing/stuffing box: compression seal prevents stem leakage

STEM DESIGNS:
• Rising stem: stem moves upward when valve opens (visual position indicator)
• Non-rising stem: stem does not move (position not visually obvious)

notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Valve Types and Applications
notes.content:
VALVE TYPE CHARACTERISTICS AND APPLICATIONS:

GATE VALVE
• Isolation only, poor throttling (full on/off only)
• Low pressure drop when fully open
• Disk moves perpendicular to flow
• Best for: isolation, infrequent operation

GLOBE VALVE
• Excellent throttling ability (disk seats at ~90° to flow)
• Higher pressure drop even when open
• Disk moves parallel to flow
• Best for: flow control, frequent operation

KEROTEST VALVE
• Packless globe valve variant
• ~1.25 turns from full open to full closed
• Critical: avoid over-torquing (crushes disk)
• Best for: compact spaces, reduced maintenance

NEEDLE VALVE
• Fine precision flow adjustment
• Long cone-shaped stem tip
• Poor isolation characteristics
• Best for: precise low-flow control

BUTTERFLY VALVE
• Large diameter, high flow, low pressure service
• Soft resilient seat
• Critical: over-torque damages the seat
• Best for: large bore, low-pressure applications

PLUG AND BALL VALVES
• Quick-acting stop/start only (cannot regulate flow)
• Minimal pressure drop effect on torque
• Best for: isolation with quick response

DIAPHRAGM VALVE
• Fluid completely separated from moving parts
• Zero stem leakage
• Best for: radioactive/corrosive fluids

CHECK VALVES
• Swing check: hinged disk, lighter D/P
• Lift check: globe-like body, higher D/P
• Stop check: can be forcibly closed by actuator
• Best for: preventing backflow

RELIEF AND SAFETY VALVES
• Relief valve: opens gradually as pressure rises (accumulation allowed)
• Safety valve: pops fully open at setpoint (little/no accumulation)
• Relief for liquids; Safety for steam/gas

VACUUM BREAKER
• Opposite of relief: protects vessel from collapse
• Admits air when internal pressure drops below atmospheric

STEAM TRAPS
• Remove condensate from steam lines
• Prevent steam loss
• Types:
  - Bellows-type: volatile liquid expansion
  - Thermostatic/impulse: flashing differential
  - Bucket-type: float-operated

notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Valve Actuators and Position Verification
notes.content:
ACTUATOR FAILURE POSITIONS:
• Fail Open: opens on loss of power/control (safety for emergency drainage)
• Fail Closed: closes on loss of power (safety for isolation)
• Fail As Is: remains in current position (most associated with Motor Operated Valves)
• Fail Safe: the position required to protect the system/equipment (varies by application)

MANUAL ACTUATORS:
• Handwheel or lever: direct mechanical operation
• Reach rods: extends control distance
• Gearing: provides mechanical advantage for higher torque
• Note: Never use a wrench on MOV handwheels (can overstress actuator)

ELECTRIC ACTUATORS:
• Motor Operated Valves (MOVs):
  - Fail As Is (holds position on power loss)
  - Gear reduction for torque multiplication
  - Declutch lever allows manual/motor engagement switching
  - Position limit switches control travel limits
  - Cannot be over-torqued with wrench
• Solenoid Operated Valves:
  - Two-position (on/off only)
  - Spring placement determines fail position
  - Fast response time

PNEUMATIC ACTUATORS:
• Gas cylinder, piston, diaphragm, or vane designs
• Quarter-turn designs for butterfly/plug valves
• Spring action determines fail position on air loss
• Common in process control systems

HYDRAULIC ACTUATORS:
• Piston-driven for high-force applications
• Spring action determines fail position on pressure loss
• Used for large or high-pressure valves

VALVE CONTROLLER:
• Automatic mode: repositions valve to maintain sensed parameter
• Manual mode: operator has direct remote control

VALVE POSITION VERIFICATION METHODS:
• Light indication: Red = Open, Green = Closed, Both lit = Mid-position/Throttled
• Travel indication: Rising stem position, local mechanical pointer
• Actual verification:
  - Visual observation of stem
  - Correct number of handwheel turns
  - Quarter-turn valves: lever parallel to pipe = open, perpendicular = closed
  - Attempt to move valve (verify shut by trying to close, verify open by trying to open)
  - NEVER open a valve just to "check" if it's open

HUMAN ERROR PREVENTION:
• Verify all local/remote indications before and after manipulation
• Follow procedures and require independent verification
• Confirm valves and instrumentation are labeled correctly
• Double-check position indicators match actual position

notes.subject: CP01
notes.title: Valve Operational Issues and Prevention
notes.content:
THERMAL BINDING:

CAUSE:
• Valve body cools faster than disk during system cooldown
• Differential thermal contraction binds seat against disk
• Can make valve impossible to reopen until reheated

PREVENTION:
• Periodically crack the valve open and re-shut during cooldown
• Use proper closing torque (avoid over-torquing)
• Allow gradual cooling when possible

PRESSURE LOCKING:

CAUSE:
• Liquid trapped in valve bonnet
• Liquid pressurizes as it heats
• Pressure buildup prevents valve opening
• Common in high-temperature systems during startup

PREVENTION:
• Provide relief or vent path on valve bonnet
• Equalize/reduce pressure buildup
• Design relief path to lower trapped pressure

OPERATING EXPERIENCE LESSONS:

1. Motor Operated Valve (MOV) Handling
   - Never use wrench on MOV handwheel for extra leverage
   - Can overstress actuator gearing/casing
   - Has caused catastrophic actuator failure

2. Manual Operation Verification
   - Reaching hard stop on handwheel does NOT confirm full travel
   - Verify full designed number of turns
   - Position limit switch not reliable sole indicator

3. MOV Manual Override Precautions
   - Manually operating past designed open limit can slip position limit switch
   - Requires leak rate/stroke time retest after manual operation
   - Document any manual override operations

KEY MAINTENANCE PRACTICES:
• Regular inspection for leakage
• Packing maintenance using backseat feature
• Periodic exercise to prevent stiction
• Monitor position indicators for consistency
• Track valve operation history

notes.subject: TH01
notes.title: Systems of Measurement and Unit Conversion
notes.content:
TWO SYSTEMS OF MEASUREMENT:

AMERICAN ENGINEERING SYSTEM (ENGLISH ENGINEERS SYSTEM):
• Force: pounds force (lbf)
• Mass: pounds mass (lbm)
• Temperature: Fahrenheit (°F)
• Pressure: psi (pounds per square inch)
• Conversion: Work in Btu = (Force in lbf × Distance in ft) / 778
• Note: Complex conversion factor (778) due to inconsistent base units

INTERNATIONAL SYSTEM (SI/METRIC):
• Force: Newtons (N)
• Mass: kilograms (kg)
• Temperature: Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K)
• Pressure: Pascal (Pa), kilopascal (kPa), or bar
• Conversion: Simpler, based on powers of 10
• Preferred in most modern engineering

UNIT CONVERSION PROCESS:

1. Identify given units and desired units
2. Write conversion factor with:
   - Given units in denominator
   - Desired units in numerator
3. Multiply original value by conversion factor
4. Cancel units and solve

Example: Convert 500 lbm to kg
• Given: 500 lbm
• Desired: kg
• Conversion factor: 1 lbm = 0.4536 kg
• Calculation: 500 lbm × (0.4536 kg / 1 lbm) = 226.8 kg

COMMON CONVERSION FACTORS:
• 1 lbm = 0.4536 kg
• 1 psi = 6.895 kPa
• °K = °C + 273.15
• °R = °F + 459.67

notes.subject: TH01
notes.title: System Types and Working Fluids
notes.content:
DEFINITION OF A SYSTEM:
• A quantity of matter or space separated from surroundings by a boundary
• Everything outside the boundary is the "surroundings"
• System interactions occur at the boundary

THREE SYSTEM TYPES:

ISOLATED SYSTEM:
• No transfer of mass across boundary
• No transfer of energy across boundary
• Theoretical ideal; real systems always exchange something
• Used for simplified analysis

CLOSED SYSTEM:
• No mass transfer across boundary
• Energy transfer (heat and work) may occur
• Most common for thermodynamic analysis
• Used for:
  - Fixed mass of gas in a cylinder
  - Sealed reactor vessel
  - Closed piping sections

OPEN SYSTEM (CONTROL VOLUME):
• Mass transfer across boundary (inlet/outlet flows)
• Energy transfer (heat and work) may occur
• Most realistic for practical equipment
• Used for:
  - Turbines (steam in, exhaust out)
  - Compressors (gas in, pressurized out)
  - Boilers (water in, steam out)
  - Pumps (liquid in, pressurized out)
  - Condensers (steam in, liquid out)
  - Pipes with flow

WORKING FLUID:
• The fluid within a system that stores or transfers energy
• Key characteristic: must be able to absorb and reject heat
• Examples:
  - Steam in power plants
  - Refrigerant in cooling systems
  - Gas in turbomachinery
  - Water in hydraulic systems

SYSTEM PROPERTIES:

INTENSIVE PROPERTIES (independent of mass):
• Pressure (P)
• Temperature (T)
• Density (ρ)
• Specific volume (v)
• Velocity
• Height
• Enthalpy (h)
• Entropy (s)
• Mass-independent measurements

EXTENSIVE PROPERTIES (dependent on mass):
• Volume (V)
• Mass (m)
• Weight (W)
• Internal energy (U)
• Enthalpy (H)
• Entropy (S)
• Total property = mass × specific property

SPECIFIC PROPERTIES:
• Intensive properties derived from extensive ones
• Calculated as: specific property = extensive property / mass
• Example: specific volume v = V/m (volume per unit mass)
• Example: specific internal energy u = U/m

notes.subject: TH01
notes.title: Pressure Concepts and Pascal's Law
notes.content:
PRESSURE FUNDAMENTALS:

DEFINITION:
• Pressure = Force / Area (P = F/A)
• Units: psi (lbf/in²), Pa (N/m²), bar, atm
• Normal atmospheric pressure = 14.7 psia = 101.3 kPa

HYDROSTATIC PRESSURE:
• Pressure from weight of fluid column above reference point
• Formula: P = ρ × g × h (density × gravity × height)
• Increases with depth
• Acts in all directions equally
• Used in manometer calculations

ABSOLUTE PRESSURE:
• Measured from perfect vacuum (zero reference)
• Units: psia (pounds per square inch absolute)
• Thermodynamic calculations require absolute pressure
• Example: 10 psig = 10 + 14.7 = 24.7 psia

GAGE PRESSURE (PSIG):
• Pressure above atmospheric
• Formula: Pgage = Pabsolute - Patmospheric
• Common pressure gauge indication
• Note: Cannot be negative (use vacuum pressure for below atmospheric)

VACUUM PRESSURE:
• Pressure below atmospheric (negative gage pressure)
• Formula: Pvacuum = Patmospheric - Pabsolute
• Example: 5 inches of mercury vacuum = system pressure 9.73 psia
• Used in condenser and pump suction applications

DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE (PSID):
• Difference in pressure between two locations
• Formula: ΔP = P₁ - P₂
• Used for:
  - Filter pressure drop
  - Pressure across valves (throttling)
  - Level measurement via differential pressure

PASCAL'S LAW:

STATEMENT:
• Pressure applied to a confined fluid at any point is transmitted undiminished throughout the fluid in all directions
• Mathematical expression: F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂

APPLICATION - HYDRAULIC PRESS:
• Input force on small piston creates pressure throughout system
• Pressure acts on larger piston producing larger output force
• Ideal system: Work In = Work Out
• Formula: F₁ × d₁ = F₂ × d₂ (force × distance)
• Pressure multiplication: (A₂/A₁) = Force multiplication ratio

PRACTICAL EXAMPLE:
• Small piston: 1 in² area, 100 lbf input
• Large piston: 10 in² area
• Pressure created: P = 100 lbf / 1 in² = 100 psi
• Output force: F = 100 psi × 10 in² = 1000 lbf
• Force multiplication: 10:1 ratio
• But distance moves inversely: large piston moves 1/10 the distance

notes.subject: TH01
notes.title: Density, Specific Volume, and Temperature Scales
notes.content:
DENSITY AND SPECIFIC VOLUME:

DEFINITIONS:
• Density (ρ): mass per unit volume (lbm/ft³ or kg/m³)
• Specific volume (v): volume per unit mass (ft³/lbm or m³/kg)
• Inverse relationship: ρ = 1/v and v = 1/ρ
• Specific volume is an intensive property (independent of mass)

EFFECT OF TEMPERATURE:
• LIQUIDS: Temperature has MUCH greater effect than pressure
  - Liquids are relatively incompressible
  - Heating causes expansion
  - Cooling causes contraction
  - Example: Water density decreases from 62.4 lbm/ft³ at 32°F to 61.9 lbm/ft³ at 100°F

• GASES: Both temperature AND pressure affect volume
  - Gases are highly compressible
  - Heating at constant pressure: volume increases (Charles' Law)
  - Pressure increase at constant temperature: volume decreases (Boyle's Law)
  - Combined effects critical for gas behavior

TEMPERATURE SCALES:

RELATIVE SCALES (reference point based):

Fahrenheit (°F):
• Freezing point of water: 32°F
• Boiling point of water: 212°F
• Interval: 180 degrees between freeze/boil
• Used in American Engineering System
• Common in US industry

Celsius (°C):
• Freezing point of water: 0°C
• Boiling point of water: 100°C
• Interval: 100 degrees between freeze/boil
• Used in SI/Metric System
• Common in scientific work
• Formula: °C = (°F - 32) / 1.8

ABSOLUTE SCALES (zero = no molecular motion):

Rankine (°R):
• Corresponds to Fahrenheit scale
• Absolute zero: 0°R = -459.67°F
• Formula: °R = °F + 459.67
• Used with American Engineering units
• Maintains 1°F per 1°R interval

Kelvin (K):
• Corresponds to Celsius scale
• Absolute zero: 0 K = -273.15°C
• Formula: K = °C + 273.15
• Used with SI/Metric units
• Maintains 1°C per 1 K interval

WHY ABSOLUTE TEMPERATURE REQUIRED:
• All thermodynamic equations require absolute temperature
• Relative scales produce wrong results (ratios fail)
• Example: 2°C is NOT twice 1°C, but 275.15 K IS 1.364 times 274.15 K
• Always convert to absolute before calculations

notes.subject: TH02
notes.title: First Law of Thermodynamics and Energy Forms
notes.content:
FIRST LAW OF THERMODYNAMICS:

STATEMENT:
• Conservation of energy: Energy is neither created nor destroyed, only converted between forms
• Energy entering a system equals energy stored in or leaving it
• E_in - E_out = ΔE_system

APPLICATION TO POWER PLANTS:

The First Law governs all energy conversions:

1. Heat Transfer in Reactor Core
   - Nuclear reactions release thermal energy
   - Heat transfers from fuel to coolant

2. Coolant to Steam Conversion
   - Heat exchanger transfers reactor heat to steam generator
   - Raises temperature and quality of feedwater

3. Turbine Work
   - Expanding steam performs mechanical work
   - Converts thermal energy to rotational mechanical energy
   - Reduces steam pressure and temperature

4. Generator Conversion
   - Turbine mechanical work rotates generator
   - Converts mechanical energy to electrical energy

5. Condenser Heat Rejection
   - Low-pressure steam exhausted to condenser
   - Condenser cooling water removes latent heat
   - Cooling water temperature rises
   - Steam condenses back to liquid

6. Pump Work
   - Condensate pump adds energy to liquid
   - Increases pressure for return to steam generator
   - Completes thermodynamic cycle

ENERGY BALANCE:
• Total energy in from reactor = Electrical power out + Heat rejected
• Efficiency = Electrical out / Total thermal in × 100%

STORED VS. TRANSIENT ENERGY:

STORED ENERGY (energy a mass HAS):
• Potential Energy (PE): due to position/height
• Kinetic Energy (KE): due to motion/velocity
• Internal Energy (U): microscopic energy (molecular motion, vibration)
• Flow Energy (PV): energy due to pressure and volume

TRANSIENT ENERGY (energy IN TRANSIT between forms):
• Heat (Q): energy transfer due to temperature difference
  - Flows from hot to cold
  - No work involved
• Work (W): energy transfer due to force acting through distance
  - Mechanical work: moving solids
  - Flow work: pushing fluid through pipes
  - Turbine/pump work: converting energy

SIGN CONVENTIONS:
• Heat added to system: Q is positive (+)
• Heat removed from system: Q is negative (-)
• Work done BY system: W is positive (+)
• Work done ON system: W is negative (-)

KEY DISTINCTION:
• Stored energy is a property (depends on state)
• Heat and work are NOT properties (depend on path/process)
• At same pressure/temperature, internal energy is the same regardless of history

notes.subject: TH02
notes.title: Energy Components and Enthalpy
notes.content:
ENERGY FORMS IN THERMODYNAMIC SYSTEMS:

POTENTIAL ENERGY (PE):
• Due to position above reference point
• Formula: PE = m × z × g / gc
  - m = mass
  - z = height above reference
  - g = gravitational acceleration (32.17 lbm-ft/lbf-s²)
  - gc = conversion constant (32.17 lbm-ft/lbf-s²)
• Example: 1 lbm at 100 ft height = 100 ft-lbf of PE
• Relevant in: pump discharge lines, elevated piping, steam riser

KINETIC ENERGY (KE):
• Due to motion/velocity
• Formula: KE = m × v² / (2 × gc)
  - v = velocity (ft/s)
• Often negligible unless velocity is very high (pipe breaks, jets)
• Relevant in: high-velocity steam in turbines, jet impaction forces

INTERNAL ENERGY (U):
• Sum of all microscopic energy forms within substance
• Includes: molecular translation, rotation, vibration
• Specific internal energy u = U/m (per unit mass)
• Changes with temperature and pressure
• Absolute value unknown; only changes (ΔU) matter
• Property of the substance (path-independent)

FLOW ENERGY (PV ENERGY):
• Energy a fluid has due to its pressure and volume
• Also called "P energy" or "flow work"
• Specific flow energy = P × v (pressure × specific volume)
• The work needed to push fluid into/out of a system
• Example: 1 lbm of steam at 100 psia occupying 4.6 ft³/lbm has PV = 100 × 4.6 = 460 Pv energy units

ENTHALPY (H):

DEFINITION:
• H = U + PV
• Enthalpy = Internal Energy + Flow Work
• Measure of a fluid's total energy content due to temperature, pressure, and volume

SPECIFIC ENTHALPY (h):
• Enthalpy per unit mass: h = u + Pv
• Units: Btu/lbm (American) or kJ/kg (SI)
• Used for all thermodynamic cycle calculations
• Found in steam tables for various pressures/temperatures
• Key advantage: accounts for both internal energy and flow work

WHY ENTHALPY MATTERS:
• Directly represents energy available from working fluid
• Used to calculate turbine work: Wturbine = hin - hout
• Used to calculate pump work: Wpump = hout - hin
• Simplifies energy balance calculations for flowing systems
• Steam tables provide direct values (don't need to calculate)

RELATIONSHIP EXAMPLE:
• Saturated steam at 100 psia has h = 1187.8 Btu/lbm
• This is the energy available per pound of steam
• If steam drops to 10 psia: h = 1143.3 Btu/lbm
• Work extracted = 1187.8 - 1143.3 = 44.5 Btu/lbm per pound of steam

notes.subject: TH02
notes.title: Heat, Work, and Power
notes.content:
HEAT (Q):

DEFINITION:
• Transient energy transferred due to a temperature difference
• Flows from hotter object to cooler object
• Spontaneous (no work required)
• NOT a property of the substance (depends on process)

CHARACTERISTICS:
• Always flows from high temperature to low temperature
• Rate depends on temperature difference and material properties
• Never stored in a substance (once transferred, it's gone)
• Path-dependent: different paths between states transfer different amounts

WORK (W):

DEFINITION:
• Transient energy from a force acting through a distance
• Formula: W = F × d (force × distance)
• Units: Btu, ft-lbf, Joules
• NOT a property (depends on how process occurs)

TWO TYPES:

1. Mechanical Work
   - Solid object being moved by force
   - Example: piston moving against pressure
   - Formula: W = F × Δx

2. Flow Work (PV Energy)
   - Energy to push/pull fluid through pipes
   - Pressure × Volume energy
   - Included in enthalpy definition (h = u + Pv)

TURBINE/PUMP WORK:
• Turbine: performs work on surroundings
  - Input: high-pressure/high-temperature fluid
  - Output: mechanical work (shaft rotation) + low-pressure/low-temperature fluid
  - Work = hin - hout (decreasing enthalpy releases work)
  - Example: Steam turbine converts steam enthalpy drop to mechanical rotation

• Pump: requires work input
  - Input: low-pressure fluid + mechanical work (motor)
  - Output: high-pressure fluid
  - Work = hout - hin (increasing enthalpy requires work)
  - Example: Condensate pump raises liquid pressure for return to boiler

POWER:

DEFINITION:
• Rate of doing work (work per unit time)
• Formula: Power = Work / Time or P = W / Δt
• Units: Btu/s, Watts (J/s), horsepower (hp)
• Conversion: 1 hp = 0.707 Btu/s = 746 Watts

APPLICATIONS:
• Turbine power = specific work × mass flow rate
  - Power (Btu/s) = (hin - hout in Btu/lbm) × (mass flow rate in lbm/s)
• Pump power = specific work × mass flow rate
• Generator power = turbine power - friction losses
• Electrical power output = generator power

ENERGY BALANCE - GENERAL FORM:

FOR A CLOSED SYSTEM WITH NO PE/KE CHANGE:
• ΔU = Q - W
• Change in internal energy = Heat added - Work done by system

REARRANGED:
• Q = ΔU + W
• Heat input = Energy storage increase + Work output

SIGN CONVENTION:
• Q positive: heat INTO system
• W positive: work BY system (doing work on surroundings)
• This convention makes energy balance intuitive

notes.subject: TH03
notes.title: States, Phases, and Phase Diagrams
notes.content:
STATE OF A SUBSTANCE:

DEFINITION:
• The condition of a substance at a specific moment
• Uniquely defined by two independent, intensive properties
• Examples: (P,T), (P,v), (T,h), (P,h)
• Once two properties are known, all other properties can be determined (via tables or equations)

PHASE:

DEFINITION:
• Describes the molecular structure and arrangement
• Four phases: Solid, Liquid, Vapor/Gas, Plasma

SOLID:
• Fixed shape and volume
• Molecules closely packed in rigid structure
• Incompressible
• Example: Ice

LIQUID:
• Fixed volume but takes shape of container
• Molecules close together but can move freely
• Nearly incompressible
• Example: Water at room temperature

VAPOR/GAS:
• No fixed shape or volume
• Molecules far apart, move randomly
• Highly compressible
• Example: Steam, air

PLASMA:
• Ionized gas (ions and electrons separated)
• High temperature/energy state
• Not relevant to typical power plant cycles

PHASE CHANGES:

• Melting: Solid → Liquid (absorbed heat at constant pressure)
  - Example: Ice → Water at 32°F and 14.7 psia
• Solidification: Liquid → Solid (rejected heat at constant pressure)
• Vaporization: Liquid → Gas (absorbed heat at constant pressure)
  - Example: Water → Steam at 212°F and 14.7 psia
• Condensation: Gas → Liquid (rejected heat at constant pressure)
• Sublimation: Solid → Gas directly (skips liquid phase)
  - Example: Dry ice → CO₂ vapor

PHASE DIAGRAM (PRESSURE-TEMPERATURE):

KEY LINES:
• Fusion line: Solid-Liquid boundary (melting/freezing)
• Vaporization line: Liquid-Gas boundary (boiling/condensation)
• Sublimation line: Solid-Gas boundary

SPECIAL POINTS:
• Triple Point: Where all three phases coexist in equilibrium
  - Water: 32.02°F and 0.088 psia
  - Only one specific P-T combination where all three phases coexist
• Critical Point: Highest T and P where liquid and gas phases can still be distinguished
  - Water: 705.4°F and 3208 psia
  - Above this point: supercritical fluid (no distinction between phases)

PHASE DIAGRAM (PRESSURE-VOLUME):

VAPOR DOME:
• Region bounded by saturated liquid and saturated vapor lines
• Extends from triple point to critical point
• Any state inside dome: two-phase mixture (liquid + vapor)
• Left of dome: compressed/subcooled liquid
• Right of dome: superheated vapor

MOLLIER DIAGRAM (ENTHALPY-ENTROPY):
• Plots enthalpy (h) vs. entropy (s)
• Constant enthalpy lines: horizontal
• Constant entropy lines: vertical
• Useful for quick estimation of steam properties
• Less accurate than steam tables but good for visual analysis

notes.subject: TH03
notes.title: Steam Quality, Sensible and Latent Heat
notes.content:
FIVE STATES OF WATER:

1. SUBCOOLED (COMPRESSED) LIQUID:
   • Liquid below saturation temperature at given pressure
   • Resistant to vaporization (higher pressure prevents boiling)
   • Very slightly compressible
   • Example: Feedwater entering a steam generator at 400°F and 2500 psia
   • Properties: liquid properties from tables below saturation line

2. SATURATED LIQUID:
   • Liquid at saturation temperature for given pressure
   • On verge of boiling (any heat addition causes vaporization)
   • All liquid molecules still condensed
   • Example: Water at 212°F and 14.7 psia (atmospheric)
   • Properties: Use "saturated liquid" columns in steam tables
   • Quality: X = 0% (no vapor present)

3. WET VAPOR (TWO-PHASE MIXTURE):
   • Mixture of saturated liquid AND saturated vapor at same temperature
   • Both phases coexist in equilibrium
   • Boiling or condensation in progress
   • Example: Steam at 100 psia containing 60% vapor, 40% liquid
   • Can only exist at saturation temperature/pressure conditions
   • Properties: interpolated between liquid and vapor values based on quality

4. SATURATED VAPOR ("DRY" STEAM):
   • 100% vapor at saturation temperature (no liquid remains)
   • On verge of superheating (any heat addition raises temperature)
   • Last drop of liquid has just evaporated
   • Example: Steam at 200 psia with all liquid evaporated
   • Properties: Use "saturated vapor" columns in steam tables
   • Quality: X = 100% (all vapor)
   • Critical for turbine inlet quality (must be >95-99% to prevent blade damage)

5. SUPERHEATED VAPOR:
   • Vapor above saturation temperature at given pressure
   • All liquid has been converted; heating continues temperature rise
   • Example: Steam at 200 psia and 600°F
   • Most flexible state (pressure independent of temperature)
   • Properties: Use "superheated steam tables" at given pressure and temperature
   • Quality: Not defined (> 100% conceptually)

HEAT TRANSFER DURING PHASE CHANGE:

SENSIBLE HEAT:
• Definition: Heat addition/removal that changes TEMPERATURE
• No phase change occurs
• Observable temperature change during process
• Example: Heating water from 70°F to 210°F (temperature rises visibly)
• Applies to: Subcooled liquid heating, superheated vapor heating/cooling
• Amount depends on specific heat and temperature change

LATENT HEAT:
• Definition: Heat addition/removal that causes PHASE CHANGE (constant temperature)
• Temperature stays constant during phase transition
• All energy goes into changing molecular structure/bonds
• Two types:

  LATENT HEAT OF VAPORIZATION (LHV/Hfg):
  • Energy needed to convert saturated liquid to saturated vapor
  • Same energy released when vapor condenses to liquid
  • Example: At 14.7 psia (212°F): 970.3 Btu/lbm needed to vaporize water
  • Decreases as pressure increases
  • Reaches zero at critical point (critical point: LHV = 0)

  LATENT HEAT OF CONDENSATION:
  • Opposite of vaporization
  • Energy released when steam condenses to liquid
  • Removes exactly the latent heat that was added during vaporization
  • Example: 970.3 Btu/lbm released when steam at 212°F condenses

STEAM QUALITY (X) AND MOISTURE CONTENT (M):

QUALITY DEFINITION:
• X = fraction of vapor in wet mixture = mvapor / mtotal
• Range: 0 (saturated liquid) to 1.0 (saturated vapor)
• Also expressed as percentage: 0% to 100%
• Example: 80% quality = 80% vapor + 20% liquid by mass

MOISTURE CONTENT:
• M = fraction of liquid in mixture = mliquid / mtotal
• Inverse relationship: M = 1 - X
• Example: 80% quality = 20% moisture

VOID FRACTION:
• Volumetric fraction of vapor (not mass fraction)
• Always higher than quality (vapor occupies much more volume than liquid)
• Important for flow calculations and separator design

IMPORTANCE OF STEAM QUALITY:
• High quality required at turbine inlet (>95-99%)
• Low quality causes blade erosion and corrosion
• Each percentage point of moisture = erosion damage
• Quality calculations:
  - h = hf + X × hfg (enthalpy at given quality)
  - v = vf + X × vfg (specific volume at given quality)
  - s = sf + X × sfg (entropy at given quality)

notes.subject: TH03
notes.title: Ideal Gas Laws and Steam Tables
notes.content:
IDEAL GAS BEHAVIOR:

CHARLES' LAW (at constant pressure):
• Gas volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature
• V/T = constant (at constant P)
• V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂
• Implication: Doubling absolute temperature doubles volume
• Example: Air heated from 300K to 600K at constant pressure doubles in volume
• Note: Must use absolute temperature (Kelvin or Rankine)

BOYLE'S LAW (at constant temperature):
• Gas volume is inversely proportional to absolute pressure
• P × V = constant (at constant T)
• P₁V₁ = P₂V₂
• Implication: Doubling pressure halves volume
• Example: Air compressed from 14.7 psia to 29.4 psia at constant temperature halves in volume

COMBINED/IDEAL GAS LAW:
• Combines both Charles and Boyle's Laws
• Equation: PV/T = nR or PV = nRT
  - P = absolute pressure
  - V = total volume
  - T = absolute temperature
  - n = number of moles
  - R = gas constant (specific to each gas)
• Specific form: P = ρ × R × T (using specific volume v = V/m)
• Or: Pv = RT (for one lbm of gas)

APPLICATIONS:
• Air and gas systems (reasonable accuracy)
• Low pressures (distant from condensation)
• High temperatures (distant from phase change)
• Does NOT work for steam near saturation

WHY IDEAL GAS LAW FAILS FOR STEAM:

• Steam behavior is MORE COMPLEX than ideal gas
• Intermolecular forces become significant
• Proximity to saturation/phase change violates ideal assumptions
• PV/T NOT constant for steam
• Real steam properties deviate significantly from ideal
• Solution: Use STEAM TABLES (experimentally derived data)

STEAM TABLES:

PURPOSE:
• Provide experimentally-measured properties of water and steam
• Account for complex non-ideal behavior
• Tabulated data for common pressures and temperatures

SATURATED STEAM TABLES:
• Indexed by saturation temperature OR saturation pressure
• Provides properties at saturation point (liquid-vapor boundary)
• Four main properties given for both liquid and vapor:
  - v (specific volume, ft³/lbm)
  - h (specific enthalpy, Btu/lbm)
  - s (specific entropy, Btu/lbm-°R)
  - u (specific internal energy, Btu/lbm)
• Additional properties:
  - Hfg (latent heat of vaporization)
  - Vfg (volume difference vapor-liquid)
  - Sfg (entropy difference vapor-liquid)

SUPERHEATED STEAM TABLES:
• Indexed by pressure and temperature
• Provides properties for steam ABOVE saturation temperature
• At given pressure, different temperatures give different properties
• Used when: P is known, T is known, and T > Tsat

COMPRESSED LIQUID TABLES:
• Properties of liquid below saturation temperature
• Often approximated by saturated liquid properties at same temperature
• Small effect of pressure on liquid properties (incompressible)

DEGREES OF SUBCOOLING:
• Difference between saturation temperature and actual liquid temperature
• Formula: Degrees of subcooling = Tsat - Tactual
• Example: Feedwater at 250 psia and 350°F
  - Tsat at 250 psia = 401°F
  - Subcooling = 401 - 350 = 51°F
  - Indicates room for temperature rise before boiling

DEGREES OF SUPERHEAT:
• Difference between actual vapor temperature and saturation temperature
• Formula: Degrees of superheat = Tactual - Tsat
• Example: Steam at 100 psia and 400°F
  - Tsat at 100 psia = 327°F
  - Superheat = 400 - 327 = 73°F
  - Indicates how far above saturation point

notes.subject: TH04
notes.title: Thermodynamic Processes and Terminology
notes.content:
PROCESS DEFINITIONS:

ADIABATIC PROCESS:
• No loss or gain of heat (Q = 0)
• Boundary is perfectly insulated
• System and surroundings exchange no thermal energy
• Common approximation for:
  - Turbine expansion (very fast, limited time for heat loss)
  - Pump compression (very fast, limited time for heat loss)
  - Compressed air systems (insulated piping)
• In reality: all real processes transfer some heat (never truly adiabatic)
• Analysis: W = ΔU (all work comes from internal energy change)

ISOBARIC PROCESS:
• Constant pressure throughout (P = constant)
• Boundary moves freely without resistance
• Heat addition/removal occurs at constant pressure
• Common examples:
  - Boiler steam generation (constant pressure)
  - Condenser steam condensation (constant pressure)
  - Pressurized water heater at fixed pressure
• Analysis: Q = ΔH (enthalpy change directly equals heat transfer)

ISENTROPIC PROCESS:
• No change in entropy (S = constant)
• Reversible adiabatic process (no friction, no heat transfer)
• Theoretical ideal (never truly achieved in real systems)
• Used as benchmark for efficiency calculations
• Real turbines/pumps compared against isentropic ideal
• Analysis: Entropy at inlet = Entropy at outlet

ISENTHALPIC PROCESS:
• No change in enthalpy (H = constant)
• Also called "throttling" process
• Occurs through partial valve opening, orifice, pipe restriction
• Common examples:
  - Pressure reducing valve
  - Pipe break with flow
  - Flow measurement orifice
• Effect: P decreases, T may decrease, entropy increases, quality may decrease
• Analysis: hin = hout, find downstream state at new pressure using Mollier diagram

ISOTHERMAL PROCESS:
• Constant temperature throughout (T = constant)
• System remains in thermal equilibrium with surroundings
• For ideal gas: PV = constant (Boyle's Law)
• In reality: slow process allowing heat transfer to maintain temperature
• Examples:
  - Isothermal compression in heat exchanger
  - Slow expansion with cooling
• Analysis: Temperature stays constant, entropy/enthalpy change depends on substance

OTHER PROCESS TYPES:
• Isochoric: constant volume (rigid container)
• Polytropic: generalized process with n = variable
• Reversible: frictionless, returns system to original state with no net change
• Irreversible: real process involving friction/losses (always increases entropy)

notes.subject: TH04
notes.title: Steady Flow Systems and Second Law of Thermodynamics
notes.content:
STEADY FLOW SYSTEM CONDITIONS:

THREE REQUIREMENTS:

1. CONSTANT MASS FLOW RATE:
   • Mass entering system per unit time = mass leaving system per unit time
   • No accumulation or depletion of mass
   • Continuity equation: ṁin = ṁout
   • Example: Turbine with 100 lbm/s steam in = 100 lbm/s exhaust out

2. CONSTANT WORKING-FLUID PROPERTIES AT ANY LOCATION:
   • Properties (P, T, h, s, v) constant over time at each point
   • May vary spatially (different at inlet vs. outlet)
   • But at each location, properties do not fluctuate
   • Example: Turbine inlet always 600 psia, 600°F (not varying)

3. CONSTANT RATES OF HEAT TRANSFER AND WORK:
   • Heat transfer Q̇ constant (not fluctuating)
   • Work transfer Ẇ constant (not fluctuating)
   • Example: Turbine power output = steady 100 MW (not pulsing)

IMPORTANCE:
• Most power plant equipment operates under steady-flow conditions
• Simplifies analysis and calculations
• Energy balance becomes straightforward:
  Q̇ + ṁ(hin) = ṁ(hout) + Ẇ
  Heat added + Enthalpy in = Enthalpy out + Work done

MECHANICAL EQUIVALENT OF HEAT (JOULE'S CONSTANT):

CONVERSION FACTOR:
• 1 Btu = 778 ft-lbf
• Also: 1 Btu = 1055 Joules
• Gc (conversion constant) = 778 lbm-ft/lbf-s² or 32.17 lbm-ft/lbf-s²
• Purpose: converts between mechanical and thermal energy units

APPLICATION:
• When calculating turbine/pump work in Btu from pressure-volume work
• Turbine work (Btu/lbm) = (pressure drop × specific volume) / 778
• Ensures consistent units throughout calculations

SECOND LAW OF THERMODYNAMICS:

STATEMENT:
• No engine, actual or ideal, operating in a cycle can convert all heat supplied into work
• A heat rejection process is ALWAYS required
• Even perfect (frictionless) engines cannot be 100% efficient
• Mathematical form: ηmax = 1 - (Tcold / Thot) using absolute temperatures

REVERSIBLE vs. IRREVERSIBLE PROCESSES:

REVERSIBLE (IDEAL/FRICTIONLESS):
• Theoretical construct only (never achieved in reality)
• No friction, no turbulence, no irreversibilities
• System could return to original state with no net change to surroundings
• Represents the BEST POSSIBLE performance
• Example: Isentropic turbine expansion
• Used as benchmark: Real efficiency = Real work / Isentropic work

IRREVERSIBLE (REAL):
• All actual processes are irreversible
• Friction losses always present
• Turbulence causes energy dissipation
• Finite temperature differences in heat transfer
• Cannot return to original state without external work/energy
• Always produces entropy increase

ENTROPY CHANGE:

DEFINITION:
• Entropy (S): measure of unavailable energy in a system
• Change in entropy: ΔS = Q / T (heat transferred / absolute temperature)
• Units: Btu/°R per unit mass (Btu/lbm-°R)
• More heat transferred at low temperature = larger entropy change
• More heat transferred at high temperature = smaller entropy change

SECOND LAW (Entropy Form):
• Total entropy must increase in any real process
• ΔS_system + ΔS_surroundings > 0 (real process)
• ΔS_system + ΔS_surroundings = 0 (reversible/ideal process)
• ΔS_system + ΔS_surroundings < 0 (impossible)

IMPLICATIONS:
• Every real process generates entropy (irreversibility)
• More entropy generation = more inefficiency
• Designers minimize irreversible entropy generation to maximize efficiency
• Entropy increases in: friction, throttling, mixing, finite temperature differences

CARNOT EFFICIENCY:
• Maximum theoretical efficiency for any cycle:
  η_Carnot = (Thot - Tcold) / Thot = 1 - (Tcold / Thot)
• Depends ONLY on source/sink temperatures (absolute)
• Independent of working fluid or cycle type
• Real cycles always operate below Carnot efficiency

notes.subject: TH04
notes.title: Heat Exchangers, Nozzles, and Flow Devices
notes.content:
HEAT EXCHANGER ENERGY BALANCE:

FUNDAMENTAL EQUATION:
• q = hout - hin
• Heat transfer = change in specific enthalpy of working fluid
• No work is done in a heat exchanger (W = 0)
• Energy balance: All heat change appears as enthalpy change
• Units: Btu/lbm (per unit mass) or Btu/s (total rate)

STEAM GENERATOR (BOILER):
• Purpose: Raises feedwater enthalpy to produce steam
• Process: Isobaric (constant pressure) heat addition
• Inlet: Subcooled liquid feedwater (low enthalpy)
• Outlet: Near-saturated or saturated steam (high enthalpy)
• Heat requirement: Qin = ṁ (hout - hin)
• Example: Feedwater at 350°F, 2500 psia enters
           Steam at 600°F, 2500 psia exits
           Heat per pound = hsteam - hwater

CONDENSER:
• Purpose: Removes latent heat of condensation
• Process: Isobaric (constant pressure) heat rejection
• Inlet: Low-pressure wet steam or saturated steam
• Outlet: Subcooled liquid condensate
• Heat removal: Qout = ṁ × Hfg (mainly latent heat)
• Critical function:
  - Condensing collapses steam volume dramatically
  - Maintains low pressure (vacuum) in turbine exhaust
  - Increases pressure drop across turbine (more work extracted)
  - Improves overall cycle efficiency
• Secondary effect: Cooling water temperature rises as it absorbs heat

CONDENSATE SUBCOOLING:
• After condensation, cooling water continues to cool liquid below saturation
• Few degrees subcooling REQUIRED to prevent pump cavitation
• Pump cavitation: Vapor bubbles form/collapse in pump impeller
  - Causes vibration, erosion, bearing damage
  - Critical to maintain condensate below saturation
• Excessive subcooling WASTES HEAT
  - Removes useful heat that could be recycled
  - Increases oxygen absorption and corrosion
• Optimal range: 10-20°F subcooling

NOZZLES:

DEFINITION:
• Shaped passages that convert pressure/enthalpy to velocity/kinetic energy
• Converts flow energy to directed jet
• No work is done (W = 0)
• No heat transfer (adiabatic)

CONVERGENT NOZZLE:
• Flow area decreases in direction of flow
• Effect:
  - Velocity INCREASES
  - Kinetic energy INCREASES
  - Pressure DECREASES
  - Temperature DECREASES
• Used for: Accelerating steam flow, creating high-velocity jets
• Formula: Continuity equation: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂
  (As area shrinks, velocity increases to maintain constant mass flow)

DIVERGENT NOZZLE:
• Flow area increases in direction of flow
• Effect:
  - Velocity DECREASES
  - Kinetic energy DECREASES
  - Pressure INCREASES
  - Temperature INCREASES
• Used for: Slowing high-velocity flow, recovering pressure

CONTINUITY EQUATION:
• Assumes constant density: Area₁ × Velocity₁ = Area₂ × Velocity₂
• If pipe area shrinks, velocity must increase (and vice versa)
• Maintains constant mass flow rate through different pipe diameters
• Critical for design of piping transitions

NOZZLE APPLICATIONS:

1. Flow Measurement
   - Orifice creates pressure drop proportional to flow rate
   - Measure differential pressure to infer flow

2. Flow Restriction
   - Nozzle restricts maximum flow rate
   - Protects equipment from excessive flow

3. Pressure Recovery
   - Divergent nozzle downstream of valve
   - Recovers some pressure after throttling
   - Improves downstream conditions

4. Turbine Nozzles (Fixed Blades)
   - Direct high-pressure steam toward rotating blades
   - Converts enthalpy to kinetic energy
   - High-velocity jets strike rotating blades efficiently

5. Steam Jet Air Ejectors (SJAE)
   - Uses convergent-then-divergent nozzle path
   - High-velocity steam jet entrains air
   - Removes non-condensable gases from condenser
   - Maintains low pressure/high vacuum

notes.subject: TH04
notes.title: Turbines, Pumps, and Throttles
notes.content:
TURBINE - WORK EXTRACTION:

FUNDAMENTAL EQUATION:
• WT = hin - hout
• Turbine work = enthalpy drop across turbine
• Positive work: high-pressure steam entering, low-pressure steam leaving
• Energy from expansion is converted to rotating mechanical energy

TWO-STEP ENERGY CONVERSION:

1. NOZZLE STAGE (Fixed Blades):
   • Converts pressure/enthalpy to kinetic energy
   • High-pressure, low-velocity steam enters nozzle
   • Convergent nozzle accelerates steam
   • Low-pressure, high-velocity jet exits toward rotating blades
   • Process: Isentropic expansion

2. BLADE STAGE (Moving Blades):
   • Jet impacts rotating blades
   • Blade motion redirects jet flow
   • Kinetic energy converted to mechanical rotation
   • Blade rotation drives turbine shaft
   • Exhaust velocity reduced but not zero

TURBINE BLADE DESIGN:

IMPULSE vs. REACTION:
• Impulse: Fixed blades do all acceleration; moving blades redirect flow
• Reaction: Moving blades continue acceleration; mixed design

MULTI-STAGING:
• Multiple sets of fixed + rotating blades
• Each stage extracts portion of total enthalpy
• Typical turbines: 6-12 stages
• One stage = one set of fixed blades + one set of moving blades
• Benefits:
  - Better efficiency through gradual expansion
  - Lower blade stresses
  - Better moisture handling in final stages
  - Optimal velocity for each stage

TURBINE EFFICIENCY:

IDEAL TURBINE WORK:
• Assumes isentropic (reversible adiabatic) expansion
• No friction losses
• No turbulence losses
• All theoretical work extracted
• Calculated as: Wideal = h1 - h2s (where s indicates isentropic)

REAL TURBINE WORK:
• Actual work output less than ideal
• Friction, turbulence, moisture losses reduce output
• Entropy increases (irreversible process)
• Lower exit velocity than ideal
• Calculated from: Wreal = h1 - h2actual

TURBINE EFFICIENCY DEFINITION:
• ηturbine = Wreal / Wideal
• Ratio of actual work to ideal work
• Typical values: 80-90% for large turbines
• Higher efficiency at design point, lower at off-design conditions
• Efficiency equation: ηt = (h1 - h2) / (h1 - h2s)

POWER CALCULATION:
• Power = Specific work × Mass flow rate
• P = (hin - hout) × ṁ
• Units: Btu/s or horsepower (1 hp = 0.707 Btu/s)
• Electrical power output = Turbine power - generator/bearing losses

PUMP - WORK INPUT:

FUNDAMENTAL EQUATION:
• WP = hout - hin
• Pump work = enthalpy rise across pump
• Negative work: motor does work ON pump, pressure increases
• Pump increases fluid pressure for return to system

IDEAL PUMP WORK:
• Assumes isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression
• No friction, no turbulence
• All input work goes to pressure increase
• Calculated as: Wideal = h2s - h1

REAL PUMP WORK:
• Actual work input more than ideal
• Friction and turbulence require extra energy
• Entropy increases (irreversible process)
• Higher temperature rise than ideal
• Calculated from: Wreal = h2actual - h1

PUMP EFFICIENCY:
• ηpump = Wideal / Wreal (opposite of turbine definition!)
• Ratio of ideal work to actual work needed
• Typical values: 75-85% for centrifugal pumps
• Efficiency equation: ηp = (h2s - h1) / (h2 - h1)
• Lower efficiency means more motor power needed

CYCLE WORK ACCOUNTING:
• Net work = Turbine work - Pump work
• Wnet = WT - WP
• Pump work typically 1-5% of turbine work
• Still significant impact on cycle efficiency

THROTTLE (THROTTLING PROCESS):

DEFINITION:
• Constant-enthalpy process through a restriction
• Examples: Partially-open valve, orifice, pipe restriction
• Conditions: No work done, no heat added

THROTTLING EFFECTS:
• Pressure DECREASES (P2 < P1)
• Enthalpy CONSTANT (h2 = h1)
• Entropy INCREASES (S2 > S1) - irreversible
• Temperature may decrease (wet steam region)
• Quality may decrease (more liquid appears)
• Velocity may increase (lower pressure)

FINDING DOWNSTREAM STATE:
• Known: Upstream state (P1, h1) and downstream pressure P2
• Find: Downstream state using h2 = h1
• Method 1: Use Mollier diagram
  - Find upstream state on h-s diagram
  - Move horizontally (constant h) to downstream pressure line
  - Read downstream temperature, quality, entropy
• Method 2: Use steam tables
  - At downstream pressure P2, find saturation enthalpy hf and hg
  - If h1 < hf: compressed liquid (subcooled)
  - If hf < h1 < hg: calculate quality X = (h1 - hf) / hfg
  - If h1 > hg: superheated (use superheat tables)

THROTTLING APPLICATIONS:
• Pressure reducing valves: Reduce system pressure for safe operation
• Flow measurement: Create differential pressure proportional to flow
• System protection: Limit flow/pressure to equipment
• Seasonal load adjustment: Reduce steam flow in low-load conditions
• Safety: Maintain acceptable downstream conditions

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Thermodynamic Cycles - Fundamentals
notes.content:
FIVE ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS OF A THERMODYNAMIC CYCLE:

1. A Working Fluid
   - The substance that operates through the cycle (typically steam or gas)
   - Must be able to absorb and reject heat
   - Properties change as it moves through different processes

2. An Engine
   - Device that converts heat energy into mechanical work
   - Examples: turbines, pistons, compressors
   - Efficiency determines how much heat is converted to work

3. A Heat Source
   - Supplies thermal energy to the working fluid
   - Examples: boiler, furnace, solar radiation
   - Temperature of heat source affects cycle efficiency

4. A Heat Sink
   - Absorbs waste heat rejected by the cycle
   - Examples: condenser, cooling tower, atmosphere
   - Lower heat sink temperature improves efficiency

5. A Device to Move the Working Fluid
   - Pump or compressor that circulates the working fluid
   - Requires work input to keep cycle operating
   - Necessary for continuous cycle operation

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: The Carnot Cycle
notes.content:
THE CARNOT CYCLE: Theoretical Maximum Efficiency Standard

FOUR PROCESSES OF CARNOT CYCLE:

1. Isothermal Expansion (1→2)
   - Temperature remains constant
   - Heat is absorbed from the heat source
   - Pressure decreases, volume increases
   - Work is output by the system

2. Adiabatic Expansion (2→3)
   - No heat transfer occurs
   - Temperature and pressure decrease
   - Working fluid expands against a turbine
   - Maximum work is extracted

3. Isothermal Compression (3→4)
   - Temperature remains constant
   - Heat is rejected to the heat sink
   - Volume decreases, pressure increases
   - Work is input to the system

4. Adiabatic Compression (4→1)
   - No heat transfer occurs
   - Temperature and pressure increase
   - Returns to initial state
   - Work is input to the system

CARNOT EFFICIENCY FORMULA:
   η = 1 - (T_cold / T_hot)

Where T_cold and T_hot are absolute temperatures (Kelvin)
Note: Only depends on temperature ratio - independent of working fluid type!

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Improving Carnot Cycle Efficiency
notes.content:
HOW TO INCREASE CARNOT EFFICIENCY:

The Carnot efficiency formula shows: η = 1 - (Tcold / Thot)

TWO WAYS TO INCREASE EFFICIENCY:

1. INCREASE THE HEAT SOURCE TEMPERATURE (Thot)
   - More energy input from higher temperature source
   - Larger temperature difference with heat sink
   - Efficiency gain is proportional to temperature increase
   - Example: Superheating steam in the boiler
   - Practical limit: Material strength at high temperatures

2. DECREASE THE HEAT SINK TEMPERATURE (Tcold)
   - Larger temperature difference with heat source
   - More work extracted per unit of heat input
   - Example: Improving condenser cooling (vacuum)
   - Practical limit: Ambient temperature (cannot go below)
   - Deep vacuum in condenser maximizes this effect

PRACTICAL CONSIDERATIONS:

For Power Plants:
• Increasing boiler temperature is limited by metallurgy
• Improving condenser vacuum is cost-effective
• Even small improvements in efficiency add up over time
• 1% efficiency improvement = significant fuel/cost savings

Real cycles (Rankine, Brayton) operate below Carnot efficiency
but follow the same principles for optimization.

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Steam Cycle Improvements - Moisture Separator Reheater
notes.content:
MOISTURE SEPARATOR REHEATER (MSR): Protecting Turbine Blades

WHY IS MOISTURE A PROBLEM IN LOW-PRESSURE TURBINES?

1. Blade Erosion
   - Moisture droplets impact blade surfaces at high velocity
   - Causes pitting, cracking, and material loss
   - Reduces blade lifespan and turbine efficiency
   - Final stage blades most vulnerable

2. Corrosion
   - Wet steam promotes oxidation of blade materials
   - Reduces structural integrity
   - May lead to blade failure and turbine damage

HOW THE MSR WORKS:

Extraction Steam Function:
• Extracts steam from intermediate turbine stage
• Uses high-temperature steam to reheat wet steam
• Evaporates moisture droplets
• Increases temperature and quality of steam

Process Steps:
1. Wet steam from LP turbine inlet flows into separator
2. Centrifugal action separates liquid droplets from steam
3. Liquid collected and drained to condenser
4. Separated steam passes through reheater tubes
5. Extraction steam heats the tubes from outside
6. Dry steam re-enters LP turbine for final expansion

BENEFITS:
• Protects final stage blades from erosion
• Improves turbine reliability and lifespan
• Maintains system safety
• Note: Does NOT significantly improve overall cycle efficiency
   (small loss from extraction point offsets any gains)

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Feedwater Heating and Cycle Efficiency
notes.content:
FEEDWATER HEATING: Improving Cycle Efficiency

BASIC CONCEPT:

Instead of returning condensed water directly to boiler,
feedwater is preheated using extraction steam from the turbine.
This improves overall cycle efficiency.

WHY DOES IT IMPROVE EFFICIENCY?

1. Reduced Heat Input Required
   • Feedwater enters boiler at higher temperature
   • Less heat needed to reach desired steam temperature
   • Lower heat addition requirement = higher efficiency
   • Formula: η = W_out / Q_in (lower Q_in increases η)

2. Less Entropy Generation
   • Preheating reduces temperature difference in boiler
   • Lower entropy generation in heat addition process
   • More reversible process = higher efficiency

HOW FEEDWATER HEATING WORKS:

1. Extraction Point
   • Steam extracted from turbine at intermediate pressure
   • Temperature = higher than feedwater, lower than inlet

2. Heat Exchange
   • Extracted steam flows through tubes
   • Feedwater flows around tubes
   • Heat transfers from extraction steam to feedwater
   • Extraction steam condenses to liquid
   • Feedwater temperature increases

3. Cycle Impact
   • Preheated feedwater returns to boiler
   • Boiler heat addition is less than without preheating
   • Total plant efficiency increases
   • Slight loss at extraction point is offset by boiler savings

EFFICIENCY GAIN:
• Typical improvement: 1-3% depending on extraction point
• More extraction points = greater improvement
• Optimized by balancing extraction vs. expansion work loss
• Modern power plants often have 3-8 feedwater heaters

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Condenser Vacuum and Cycle Efficiency
notes.content:
CONDENSER VACUUM: Critical Factor in Efficiency

WHAT IS CONDENSER VACUUM?

• The pressure inside the condenser is lower than atmospheric
• Expressed as absolute pressure (psia) or as inches of mercury
• Example: 1 psia absolute = about 29 inches of mercury vacuum
• Lower absolute pressure = higher vacuum = better efficiency

WHY DOES LOWER PRESSURE INCREASE EFFICIENCY?

1. Increased Expansion Ratio
   • Pressure drop across turbine: from boiler pressure to condenser pressure
   • Lower condenser pressure = larger expansion ratio
   • More work is extracted per unit of steam
   • Turbine does more work with same steam flow

2. More Work Output
   • Example comparison:
     - At atmospheric pressure condenser: less work extracted
     - At vacuum condenser: more work extracted from same steam
     - Extra work directly increases efficiency

3. Formula Relationship
   • Carnot efficiency: η = 1 - (T_cold / T_hot)
   • Lower pressure = lower temperature of condenser
   • Larger T_hot/T_cold ratio = higher efficiency

IMPROVING CONDENSER VACUUM:

1. Cooling Water Flow
   • More/cooler cooling water improves heat rejection
   • Lowers steam condensation temperature
   • Maintains lower absolute pressure

2. Air Removal
   • Vacuum pump removes air from condenser
   • Air reduces effective pressure
   • Regular maintenance keeps pump effective

3. Condenser Tube Cleaning
   • Fouling reduces heat transfer
   • Clean tubes allow better cooling
   • Regular cleaning improves vacuum

4. Temperature Management
   • Cooling water temperature affects vacuum
   • Seasonal variations impact performance
   • Cold water = better vacuum = higher efficiency

PRACTICAL IMPACT:

• Each inch of mercury improvement in vacuum:
  - Increases efficiency by ~0.5-1.0%
  - Increases power output
  - Major focus area for power plant optimization
  - Cost-effective improvement strategy
  - Monitored continuously by operators

notes.subject: TH05
notes.title: Heat Rate and Plant Efficiency
notes.content:
HEAT RATE: Understanding Power Plant Performance

WHAT IS HEAT RATE?

Heat Rate = Energy Input (BTU) / Electrical Output (kW-hr)

Units: BTU/kW-hr (typical range: 7,000-10,000 for modern plants)

EXAMPLE:
• A plant with heat rate of 8,000 BTU/kW-hr
• To produce 1 kW-hr of electricity
• Requires 8,000 BTU of fuel energy input
• Waste: 8,000 - 3,412 BTU = 4,588 BTU lost as waste heat
  (Note: 1 kW-hr = 3,412 BTU)

RELATIONSHIP TO EFFICIENCY:

1. Inverse Relationship
   • Lower heat rate = Higher efficiency
   • Higher heat rate = Lower efficiency
   • Directly inversely proportional

2. Conversion Formula
   • Efficiency (%) = 3,412 / Heat Rate × 100
   • Example: 3,412 / 8,000 × 100 = 42.65% efficiency

3. Thermodynamic Limit
   • Theoretical minimum heat rate depends on temperatures
   • Carnot limit: Heat Rate = 3,412 / (η_Carnot × 100)
   • Real plants operate at 50-75% of Carnot efficiency

WHY HEAT RATE MATTERS:

1. Economic Impact
   • 1% improvement = significant fuel cost savings
   • 1 MWe plant over 20 years: $5-10M savings
   • Direct impact on power generation cost

2. Environmental Impact
   • Lower heat rate = less fuel burned
   • Reduced CO2 and emissions per MWh produced
   • Direct contribution to emissions reduction

3. Operational Indicator
   • Real-time measure of plant performance
   • Indicates maintenance needs
   • Trending helps predict problems
   • Used to optimize operations

IMPROVING HEAT RATE:

• Condenser vacuum improvements
• Feedwater heating optimization
• Moisture separator reheater tuning
• Reducing auxiliary power consumption
• Regular maintenance and cleaning
• Operator skill and experience

INDUSTRY BENCHMARKING:

• Best-in-class coal plants: ~8,200 BTU/kW-hr
• Average coal plants: ~9,000-9,500 BTU/kW-hr
• Older plants: >10,000 BTU/kW-hr
• Combined cycle plants: ~6,500-7,000 BTU/kW-hr
• Modern gas turbines: ~7,500-8,500 BTU/kW-hr
